full_text: So we know this invasion will fail just based on six fundamental problems. Okay? So even if you don't really know military strategy, these six problems will tell you exactly why American ground troops cannot succeed in Iran. First of all, this is not Iraq 2003. Turkey, a NATO ally, has made the same calculation. Erdogan isn't risking Turkish cities getting hit by Iranian missiles to help America invade Iran. The Gulf states are publicly neutral and privately terrified. So, American forces would have to stage from aircraft carriers and from the limited bases they still have access to in the Gulf. Which means the logistics chain, the supply line that keeps half a million soldiers fed, armed, fueled, and medically supported, runs across thousands of miles of ocean and hostile territory under constant threat of Iranian missile and drone strikes. That's not a difficult logistics challenge. That's a logistics nightmare that gets worse every single day the invasion continues. And the soldiers know it. full_text: So, let me tell you something that most people get completely wrong when they think about military power. They assume that the strongest army always wins. That the country with the biggest budget, the most advanced weapons, the most aircraft carriers, that country will always come out on top. And on the surface, that logic makes total sense. Why wouldn't it? But here's where it gets interesting. And honestly, this idea flipped my entire understanding of how wars actually work. The United States has the most powerful military in the history of human civilization. I am not exaggerating when I say that. The budget alone is larger than the next 10 countries combined. The technology is generations ahead of most rivals. The global network of bases, the satellite intelligence systems, the nuclear arsenal, the carrier strike groups, it is genuinely unlike anything the world has ever seen before. If you put America in a direct conventional war against almost any other nation on earth, it is not even a close contest. It will be over very quickly. And yet, and yet, Iran is still standing. Iran has been under enormous pressure from Washington for decades. Sanctions, threats, proxy conflicts, assassinations of top military figures, and still the Iranian government operates. Still, the Iranian military builds and develops. Still, Thran refuses to simply collapse the way Washington would prefer. How does that happen? How does a country with a fraction of the military budget, fall older equipment, and no nuclear deterrent manage to survive in a permanent standoff with the most powerful nation in history? The answer is that Iran is not trying to win the way America defines winning. Iran is playing an entirely different game. And once you understand that distinction, everything about their strategy starts to make sense. Think about it this way. Imagine two people in a fight. One person is a professional heavyweight boxer, big, strong, fast, trained for years. The other person is smaller, lighter, far less powerful in a direct confrontation. If they fight in a boxing ring following boxing rules, the smaller person loses every single time. It is not even close. But now imagine the fight moves out of the ring, into a dark alley, into a crowded market, into a building the smaller person has lived in for 20 years and knows like the back of their hand. Suddenly the rules change. Suddenly the boxer's power and training become less relevant. And suddenly the person who knows every corner of that environment has a real advantage. That is the concept of asymmetrical warfare. And it is not a new idea. weaker powers have been using it against empires for thousands of years. The idea is simple. You do not fight on the stronger powers terms. You reshape the battlefield until their strengths become liabilities. And here is something that makes this more than just theory. In 2002, the United States military actually ran a massive simulation to test exactly this scenario. They called it Millennium Challenge 2002. It was one of the largest war games ever conducted. The whole point was to simulate a conflict between American forces and a Middle Eastern adversary that operated a lot like Iran. They divided into two teams. Blue team was America, red team was the adversary. And in the early stages of the simulation, the red team commander did something that completely blindsided the Pentagon. Instead of meeting the American naval force directly, he used swarming tactics, small fast boats, unconventional missile attacks, irregular communication methods that the Americans could not easily track or jam. Surprise, speed, chaos. Within hours, the simulation showed that a massive portion of the American fleet had been destroyed. Thousands of simulated casualties, billions in simulated losses. The most powerful navy in the world have been defeated by an opponent who refused to play by conventional rules. Now, here is where the story gets even more reviewing. The Americans restarted the exercise, but this time they changed the rules. They told the Red Commander he was no longer allowed to use those asymmetrical tactics. He had to fight in a more traditional predictable way. And when the game resumed under those conditions, America won easily. So, what does that tell us? It tells us that American military dominance is real and overwhelming. But only when the war is fought on American terms. The moment an adversary refuses to accept those terms, the entire equation shifts. Iran has studied this. Iran has studied Vietnam where America had overwhelming superiority in technology and firepower and still could not convert that into a decisive victory because the Vietnamese refused to fight the expected way. Iran has studied Afghanistan where the most sophisticated military machine in history spent 20 years in a conflict it could not resolve against fighters using basic weapons and a deep knowledge of local terrain. Iran has studied every case where a powerful empire ran into the limits of raw military force. And from all of that study, Iran has developed what you might call a strategic matrix. a framework where every action, every alliance, every military investment, every diplomatic move must serve multiple purposes at the same time. Because here's the reality. Iran cannot afford to waste resources. If you are a smaller power facing a superpower, every single move has to be efficient. You cannot make gestures. You cannot spend political capital on actions that only serve one purpose. Everything has to work on at least two or three levels simultaneously. So what are those levels? What is Iran actually trying to achieve with this matrix? The first thing is internal cohesion. If a conflict begins, if bombs start falling or American ships enter the Gulf in an aggressive posture, Iran needs its population to unite rather than fracture. And this matters enormously. One of the ways that outside powers break regimes is by turning the population against its own government during a crisis. If the Iranian people believe they are under attack, survival instinct kicks in. National identity becomes the dominant force. The government becomes harder to destabilize from within. So every action Iran takes is partly designed to reinforce the idea that this is a fight for survival, not a fight between competing political systems. The second level is alliance building. Iran cannot stand alone. No country can. And so for decades, Iran has been cultivating relationships across the region and beyond. Hezbollah in Lebanon, the Houthis in Yemen, various militia networks in Iraq and Syria, relationships with Russia and China at the diplomatic level. These are not random. Each relationship is a node in the network designed to complicate American calculations. If attacking Iran means triggering responses across multiple countries simultaneously, the cost of that attack increases dramatically. Suddenly, Washington is not just thinking about one conflict. It is managing a region on fire. The third level is the battle for global opinion. This is one the United States often underestimates and Iran takes very seriously. Wars in the modern era are not only won on battlefields. They are won or lost in the court of international public opinion, at the United Nations, in the media, in the streets of cities around the world where people protest or support. If Iran can consistently frame any conflict as a case of a smaller country defending itself against imperial aggression, the political cost for Washington goes up. Congressional support becomes harder to maintain. Allied countries become reluctant to participate. The moral narrative matters. And the fourth level is strategic attrition. Not destroying America, not humiliating America in some single dramatic confrontation, but exhausting America. Slowly raising the cost of engagement over time. Making the calculation in Washington shift from we can win this to is this worth it? Economic disruption through pressure on oil shipping routes. Psychological pressure through the threat of missile attacks on American bases in the region. the constant low-level friction that drains resources, attention, and political will. Now, here is the key insight. This entire strategy depends on one concept more than any other, and that concept is patience. America is a democracy. American presidents face elections every four years. Public opinion shifts. Media cycles move fast. Budgets are debated annually. The political system creates pressure for quick results. Voters do not want to hear that a conflict might take a decade to resolve. Iran is playing on a completely different time scale. The Iranian strategic establishment thinks in terms of decades. They have been developing this approach since the revolution in 1979. They are not looking for a quick win. They are looking for a situation where America eventually decides the cost of continued pressure is too high. Where Washington calculates that the resources being spent to maintain maximum pressure on Iran could be better used elsewhere. where the political will in the United States simply runs out. And if you look at history, that is actually how many asymmetrical conflicts end, not with the weaker side winning a decisive battle, but with the stronger side exhausting itself and withdrawing. There is another factor that makes the Iranian matrix so unsettling to analysts in Washington, and that is the economic asymmetry of modern weapon systems. American aircraft carriers cost $5 billion to build. The aircraft that fly off them cost hundreds of millions each. The systems that protect those carriers are enormously expensive. And Iran has spent years developing relatively cheap drones, missiles, and fast boat swarms that can threaten those assets. Not with certainty, not reliably, but enough to force America to spend enormous resources on defensive systems to protect enormously expensive hardware from relatively inexpensive threats. When the cost of defending something is much higher than the cost of threatening it, the mathematics of warfare start to shift. The stronger power starts bleeding money in ways that are difficult to sustain politically over long periods. And this is the endgame Iran is aiming at. Not a dramatic military victory, but a strategic environment where the cost of confronting Iran consistently outweighs the benefit. Where Washington ultimately concludes that some form of accommodation is preferable to permanent draining conflict. The most important thing to understand about all of this is the timing. The strategy does not begin when the shooting starts. It begins years before. Every alliance built, every missile developed, every diplomatic relationship cultivated, every narrative crafted for international audiences, all of it is preparation. By the time any actual conflict becomes visible to the world, the strategic environment has already been shaped over years or even decades. That is why analysts who look only at the balance of conventional military forces are missing the point entirely. Raw military power matters. It matters enormously, but it does not operate in a vacuum. It operates inside a strategic environment that has been built piece by piece over a very long time. And in that environment, survival is victory. Endurance is winning. If Iran is still standing, still functioning, still exerting influence across the region 5 years or 10 years or 20 years from now, that is the win. That was always the win. The question was never whether Iran could defeat America. The question was always whether Iran could survive America. And so far, against all of the pressure that has been applied, the answer has been yes. That is what makes this strategy so genuinely fascinating and so genuinely frightening to the people in Washington whose job it is to think about these

full_text: So what we're witnessing right now is a strategic masterclass in asymmetric warfare. Iran's objective isn't to defeat the United States military in a conventional sense. That the United States has no choice but to withdraw. And in the process of creating this regional chaos, Iran is actually unifying its own population around a common cause. And from a game theory perspective, this creates a massive problem for the United States. You can't win by targeting leadership because leadership is distributed. You can't predict their next move because decision-m is decentralized. And you can't negotiate with a single authority because authority is dispersed. On the surface, what's happening in Bahrain looks like an internal uprising. The timing and orchestration suggests something much more sophisticated. What we're seeing is that Israel has been working to destabilize Bahrain, specifically to force Saudi Arabia into a conflict that distracts them from the larger war with Iran. Israel has been using covert operations to amplify tensions in Bahrain. They're providing intelligence to opposition groups. They're helping organize protests. They're creating the conditions for regime change. Now, why would Israel do this? Because Saudi Arabia has a defense pact with Bahrain. The two countries are deeply interconnected. The Saudi military has bases in Bahrain. They can't let a Shia uprising succeed in Bahrain because it would set a precedent and embolden Shia populations across the Gulf. So Saudi Arabia is forced to send troops into Bahrain to suppress the uprising. They have to divert military resources, intelligence assets, political attention, all to deal with this crisis in Bahrain. And while they're focused on Bahrain, they're not focused on Iran. That's the trap. The moment Saudi Arabia commits forces to Bahrain, Iran has justification to escalate. The moment Saudi forces cross into Bahrain in significant numbers, Iranian missiles start hitting Saudi oil facilities. And I'm not talking about minor disruptions. I'm talking about the systematic destruction of Saudi Arabia's oil production capacity. Saudi Arabia has concentrated its oil infrastructure in the eastern province. Places like Akai, Kuray, Gowirfield. These facilities are massive and they're vulnerable. We already saw in 2019 when Houthi drones which are Iranian made struck Abkike. That single attack took out half of Saudi oil production temporarily. That was a warning shot. But in a full-scale war scenario, Iran wouldn't just damage these facilities, they would destroy them. This would be the worst military defeat for the United States since Vietnam, possibly worse than Vietnam in terms of the scale and speed of the collapse. Now, politically, this creates a massive problem for Trump. How do you explain to the American people that you started a war that resulted in this level of military losses? How do you maintain credibility as a strong leader when your military is being defeated? The answer is deflection and blameshifting. Trump would blame Arab leaders. He'd say, "We went to war to protect Saudi Arabia, UAE, the Gulf States. We sacrificed our blood and treasure for them, and they didn't fight. They didn't support us adequately. They let us down. This defeat is their fault, not Earth's." This narrative serves multiple purposes. It deflects responsibility from Trump personally. It provides a scapegoat for American losses. And it prepares the ground for withdrawal by saying, "We're not abandoning the region. The region abandoned us." Right? So, this is the endgame scenario that I think becomes increasingly likely as the conventional war drags on. Iran has always maintained that they're not seeking nuclear weapons, that their nuclear program is purely for peaceful purposes. But the strategic reality is that the only guarantee of regime survival in the face of American hostility is nuclear deterrence. Look at the examples. North Korea has nuclear weapons and the United States hasn't invaded. Libya gave up its nuclear program and Gaddafi was overthrown and killed. Ukraine gave up its nuclear weapons in exchange for security guarantees and then Russia invaded. The lesson is clear. If you want regime security, you need nuclear weapons. So, I'm predicting that at a certain point in this conflict, probably after the US military suffers significant losses and after the GCC starts collapsing, Iran will make a strategic decision to accelerate their nuclear weapons program. And they won't do it alone. They'll get direct assistance from Russia and North Korea. Russia has every incentive to help Iran get nuclear weapons quickly. A nuclear Iran permanently complicates US strategic planning. It makes regime change impossible. It creates another nuclear power that's aligned with Russia against Western interests. So, Russia would provide technical assistance, maybe even materials and components that would speed up the process. North Korea has similar incentives. They want to demonstrate that their nuclear technology is exportable, that they can assist allies, and they want to create more nuclear powers to dilute American focus and resources. If America is worried about Iranian nukes in addition to North Korean nukes, that's less pressure on North Korea. So, what I'm envisioning is Russian and North Korean nuclear engineers being flown into Iran. They would work at Iranian nuclear facilities, probably underground facilities that are hardened against air strikes, and they would help Iran produce enough weapons material for several nuclear devices within a matter of months rather than years. This is the nightmare scenario for the United States and Israel because once Iran has nuclear weapons, the entire strategic calculation changes. You can't do regime change anymore. You can't invade. You can't even threaten invasion credibly. Iran becomes essentially untouchable. More than that, they would test them live on national television. This would be an absolutely unprecedented move in nuclear history. Every other nuclear power has conducted tests somewhat secretly, or at least not as live broadcast spectacles. But Iran would do it differently. Here's the strategic logic. Iran's biggest domestic challenge has always been national unity. You have Persians, Azeris, Kurds, Arabs, Balois, different ethnic groups. You have religious diversity. You have generational divides. The Iranian government's legitimacy has always been somewhat contested, especially after the 2009 Green Movement protests. But a live nuclear test would create a massive unifying moment. It would be framed as Iran standing up to the great Satan. It would be presented as Iran joining the nuclear powers club, achieving parody with the United States, Israel, Russia. It would be a moment of intense national pride. So, I'm envisioning a scenario where Iranian state television broadcasts live from somewhere in eastern Iran, probably the desert areas near the Afghan border. They've built a test site there. And they announce to the world, we are now a nuclear power. We will conduct a test at such and such time. The world is invited to watch. and then they detonate a device live on TV with cameras from multiple angles, maybe even allowing international journalists to film from a safe distance. The message is this is not a bluff. This is real. We have nuclear weapons and we're not afraid to demonstrate it. The domestic impact would be enormous. Iranians watching this would feel pride, fear, excitement, patriotism all at once. It would create a rally around the flag moment that would unify the population behind the regime more effectively than any propaganda campaign ever could. Internationally, this triggers World War II rhetoric immediately. The United States, Israel, European powers, they all start talking about WW3. The UN Security Council holds emergency meetings. There's talk of global nuclear disarmament. It becomes the dominant story globally. But here's the thing. Once Iran has demonstrated nuclear capability publicly, there's nothing anyone can do about it militarily. You can't uninvent their nuclear program. You can't bomb it away at that point. The materials and knowledge are too distributed. You have to negotiate with a nuclear Iran. That's the new reality. So this is what I see as the United States final desperate attempt at regime change after all other strategies have failed. The conventional military campaign hasn't worked. The economic pressure hasn't worked. Internal opposition hasn't overthrown the regime. So the CIA goes back to an old playbook. Arm a minority group and try to create a breakaway region. The Kurds are the obvious choice. There are somewhere between 8 to 10 million Kurds in Iran, concentrated in the western and northwestern parts of the country. They have a history of seeking autonomy or independence. They have armed groups that have been fighting the Iranian government on and off for decades. And crucially, they have ethnic and political connections to Iraqi Kurdistan and Syrian Kurdistan, which creates potential for external support. So, what I'm predicting is that the CIA begins covertly transferring the remaining US military assets in the region to Kurdish groups in Iran. These would be weapons, ammunition, intelligence equipment, communications gear. Basically, everything the US can't evacuate from its collapsing bases in Iraq and Syria. The CIA would work with Kurdish militant groups like PJ, that's the Party for Free Life in Kurdistan. They would provide training, intelligence, strategic guidance. The goal would be to create a Kurdish uprising in western Iran that could potentially break off and form an autonomous region or even an independent state. And crucially, there would be American boots on the ground, not regular military. Those couldn't be deployed without congressional approval. That wouldn't come. but special forces operating covertly. CIA paramilitaries, private military contractors, probably a few thousand personnel total, working under the cover of advising Kurdish forces, but actually participating in combat operations. The strategic vision here is to create a Kurdish autonomous region in Western Iran that would be pro-American. This region would control some of Iran's oil resources in the western provinces. It would create a permanent hostile entity on Iran's borders. It would demonstrate that regime change is possible, just not for the whole country, only for parts of it. But here's why I think this strategy will ultimately fail as well. Iran has dealt with Kurdish insurgencies for decades. They know how to suppress them. They have elite IRGC units specifically trained for counterinsurgency in mountainous terrain. And most importantly, a Kurdish uprising would actually help unify the rest of Iran. You see, Persian nationalism is still the dominant force in Iranian identity. If Kurds start breaking away with American help, that triggers Persian nationalist sentiment. We're not going to let Americans tear our country apart. Even Iranians who oppose the current regime would rally behind defending Iran's territorial integrity. So the Kurdish strategy becomes another trap. It costs the United States money, resources, and lives. It probably results in some Kurdish militants being killed when Iran cracks down. But it doesn't achieve regime change. It just creates more chaos, more violence, more justification for Iranian hardline policies. So, let me tie all these threads together because this is really the genius of Iran's strategy, even if it's a dark genius. What we're witnessing is Iran deliberately creating chaos across multiple countries simultaneously. Bahrain is in regime crisis. Saudi Arabia's oil fields are destroyed. Their economy is collapsing. UAE's reputation as a safe haven is shattered by the Burge Khalifa strike. US military bases across the region are destroyed. The petro dollar system is breaking down. Energy prices are causing economic crisis in Europe and inflation crisis in America. There's potential civil unrest in the United States and talk of World War II globally. All of this is fragmenting. The Middle East is breaking into pieces. the aligned structures that held the region together, the US security umbrella, the GCC cooperation, the anti-Iran coalition, all of these are failing under the pressure. But while the region fragments, Iran unifies. Every external attack, every missile strike, every threat, it all serves to rally the Iranian population behind their government. The nuclear test becomes this massive moment of national pride. Even Iranians who don't like their government start to think, "Well, at least we're standing up to America. At least we're not puppets like the Gulf States." The economic hardship in Iran, and there is significant hardship, gets reframed as the price of independence. Yes, we're suffering, but we're suffering with dignity. We're not on our knees begging America for protection like the Saudis. The Kurdish insurgency, rather than fracturing Iran, actually strengthens Persian nationalist identity. We must defend our homeland against foreign backed separatists. So Iran emerges from this conflict more unified, more cohesive, more confident than before. They've survived the worst the United States and Israel could throw at them. They've achieved nuclear status. They've demonstrated they can project power across the region. They've shown they can bring the American military to its knees economically, if not militarily. Meanwhile, the rest of the Middle East is in ruins. The GCC states are economically devastated. Iraq and Syria are further destabilized. US influence in the region is shattered. The whole postw World War II order in the Middle East, the one built on American hegemony and oil, dollar recycling and security guarantees, that order is over. And Iran didn't have to win conventionally to achieve this. They just had to make the cost of defeating them higher than anyone was willing to pay. They had to make the Middle East ungovernable under US leadership. They had to create so much chaos that the only rational response was withdrawal. This is asymmetric warfare taken to its logical conclusion. You don't defeat the stronger power militarily. You make victory impossible to define and too expensive to achieve. You turn the enemy's strengths into weaknesses. You use their precision weapons against expensive targets to drain their resources. You use their alliances against them by creating situations where allies become liabilities. And in the end, Iran stands unified while the region around them burns. They've broken the Middle East to unite themselves. That's the strategy. And based on everything I'm seeing, it's

His family was upper middle class. His mom was on the board of directors with IBM executives, which is how he got his first big contract. And when he dropped out, he could always go back. He had a safety net. That garage, it wasn't in a poor neighborhood. Does that make sense? Look at any quote unquote self-made billionaire, and you'll find the same pattern. Family money, elite education, social connections, safety nets, they all had significant advantages. But we don't want to see this because we want to believe in the myth. We want to believe that they succeeded purely because they're geniuses who worked hard. Why? Because if we admit they had advantages, then we have to admit that maybe the system isn't fair. Maybe their wealth isn't purely deserved. And that's uncomfortable. Okay? So, we tell ourselves they're self-made geniuses. And we ignore all the evidence that says otherwise. And this brings us to a really important point about why the system stays the way it is. Look, I was born poor. I lucked into Yale. Okay, it's luck. You can work out. You can work as hard as you want, but the chances are are against you. Okay, and it takes a certain personality, which is you're really individualistic. You're very ambitious and you have high risk tolerance. Most people don't have that. Okay? So yes, there are certain people who despite their background, they're able to succeed. But that's often the exception to the rule as opposed to the rule itself. Okay? So luck is a form of strategy. Okay? Strategy and all this is saying is that you are trying to position yourself in a place that allows you to get lucky. Okay? So I left Canada and I went to United States because I knew that in Canada, doesn't matter how hard you work, no one cares. But in the United States, if you work really hard, then you might get lucky and you might meet someone who's like, "Wow, you're a really hard worker. I want to promote you." Okay? So, so you're right in that yes, you can strategize and increase your luck, but it's still luck. All right? And those who succeed are able to think strategically and far ahead and position themselves in a way that allows them to get lucky. Okay? And they often have the characteristics that we talked about, you know, like so self-control, resilience, and the ability to look inward and better understand what you're good at. Okay? So, it takes a combination of factors. But the point I'm trying to make is that these people are only a minority or a minority. Okay? 1% of the population really. Okay? And they still depend on luck. Any more questions before I move on? Okay. So, this leads us to a question which is okay. Well, the way that I frame everything, okay, the way that I frame everything, you have poor and you have rich. And as discussed, they live in their own world worlds. And they don't really connect with each other. They have different parenting strategies. They have different outlooks. The rich stay with themselves. The poor stay amongst themselves. So, this looks like a very stable system, right? Stability or the word we can use is equilibrium. But here's the really important question. Why does the system stay stable? Why doesn't it change? And the answer is something called meritocracy. Okay, meritocracy is the belief that we live in a fair system where people succeed based on their merit, their talent, their hard work. And this belief is really, really important for keeping the system stable. Let me explain why. If you're rich, you need to believe that the system is fair. Because if the system isn't fair, then your wealth is illegitimate, right? Like if you admit that you only succeeded because your parents were rich, because you went to good schools, because you had connections. If you admit all that, then you have to admit that maybe you don't deserve what you have. Maybe you're not special, maybe you just got lucky. And that's really uncomfortable. Okay? So rich people convince themselves that they earned everything. I worked hard. I'm talented. I'm smart. That's why I succeeded. They have to believe this. It's a psychological necessity. And this is why you hear rich people say things like, "I'm self-made." Even when they clearly had advantages. They need to believe in meritocracy because otherwise they have to face the fact that the system is rigged in their favor. Does that make sense? But here's the really insidious part. Poor people also believe in meritocracy. Okay? And when poor people believe that the system is fair, what does that mean? It means that if you're poor, it's your fault. You didn't work hard enough. You're not smart enough. You're not talented enough. So poor people internalize their failure as a personal flaw rather than a systemic problem. They blame themselves instead of blaming the system. And this keeps them from organizing, from demanding change, from challenging the hierarchy. Okay, there's an alternative way of thinking about this called luck egalitarianism. Okay, luck egalitarianism says we should acknowledge the role of circumstances. We should admit that where you're born, who your parents are, what opportunities you have, that all of this is luck. And if it's luck, then maybe we should try to equalize things a bit. Maybe we should redistribute resources to compensate for bad luck. But we don't do this because we're too invested in the meritocracy myth. We want to believe that success is earned and failure is deserved. And this is why the system is in equilibrium. The rich believe they deserve their wealth, so they don't feel guilty and they resist redistribution. The poor believe they deserve their poverty, so they don't rebel and they keep trying to quote unquote work harder. Everyone is locked into their position. The hierarchy reproduces itself generation after generation. And it all depends on this shared belief in meritocracy, a belief that, as we've seen throughout this lecture, is fundamentally false. Okay. So what we have is a system that looks fair on the surface. We say everyone has equal opportunity. Anyone can succeed if they work hard. But underneath it's a rigged game. The rich and the poor are playing by different rules in different worlds. And the outcome is predetermined before the game even starts. And the belief in meritocracy, that's what keeps everyone playing the game instead of flipping over the table. Does that make sense, guys? Okay.

full_text: Today's guest is Professor Jang. He's a prominent educator and writer. He was born in China in the 70s, but later [music] immigrated to Toronto, Canada, where he grew up. He graduated with distinction in English literature from Yale College, but eventually returned to China, where he taught at some of the most prestigious educational institutions [music] where he advocated for reform in greater creativity. His later work expanded into geopolitical commentary and predictive history. His work was widely respected in the academic and journalistic world, but recently Jen also exploded in popularity on social media when one of his videos predicting the Iran war went viral on YouTube. In less than a month, Jeng's YouTube channel went from obscurity to half a million subscribers with viewers pouring through his catalog of videos making all sorts of geopolitical predictions such as the Trump re-election, its implications on the Russia Ukraine war, and the event. With his newfound popularity, Professor Jen hopes to expand the work of his YouTube channel, Predictive History, where he hopes to help humanity gain more control over the present by understanding its past and estimating the future. So, it is my pleasure and honor to introduce to you all today, Professor Jang, so we can discuss [music] what the wars in the Middle East, Eastern Europe, and potentially East Asia tell us about the future of human civilization. Professor, welcome to the channel. >> Thank you so much for inviting me. So, professor, if you could just please explain to my audience what predictive history is and why you believe this field of study is so important to humanity. >> Yeah, sure. So, um if you read history books, you will um see there are many divergent views out there. Um there's like 10,000 ways of understanding anything. Um and so the idea of predictive history is let's try to systemize this. Let's try to create a system in which we can know if we're right or wrong about the way we understand history. And so my argument is that if history is done correctly, um if the framework for understanding of history is correct, then we should be able to do three things. The first is we should be able to connect the past, which is to say we should be able to turn uh the history into a coherent narrative about how we came into being. Second um is we should be able to explain the present. Uh why is the world the way it is today? The third thing is to be able to predict future events to break predict history. So if the framework for history is able to accomplish all three then we know that we're approaching the truth. Now the idea is that in every conception of history there are values and assumptions embedded in that anical model. And if we understand the values and assumptions, we can then make a prediction model based on these values and assumptions. So predictive history, it's a way for us to better understand ourselves so that we can have better control over our own lives and it's a way to um basically push history forward so that historians come to an agreement on major historical events. So that's the general gist. So what is it that you understood about the past and the present moment that helped you predict correctly the 12-day Iran Israel war? >> Right. So what clued me in that the conflict would arise between uh the United States and Iran is that in January 20 uh 20 um Donald Trump when he in in his first term he ordered an assassination of General Solommani. um the Iranian de facto ambassador in the Middle East and um that was an extremely important geopolitical event and I don't think that people really understood how important it was because historically in that region if you killed an ambassador that's a direct provocation of war. Um Fidities sorry no not Fidity but Herod Heroditus writes about this in his book the histories. Well the reason why Persia launched this massive invasion of the Greek man was because Athens and Sparta killed their ambassadors. They literally threw their ambassadors into the well. So that sort of clue me in um that a war would arise. And then what what I did next was okay, who are the actors pushing for war and why would a war happen? title: The Iran Trap: How Geography Destroys Superpowers | Prof. Jiang Xueqin

full_text: If the United States ever launches the  ground invasion of Iran, the first question any serious analyst must ask is not whether  American forces can win the opening battles. They almost certainly would, but rather where  exactly that army gets trapped. Because most people imagine war as something fast and decisive.  They imagine tanks racing across deserts, fighter jets dominating the sky, and within weeks the  capital falls and the enemy government collapses. That is the picture people still carry from the  invasion of Iraq in 2003 when American forces reach Baghdad in just 3 weeks. But the situation  with Iran is completely different. And the trap waiting for any invading force is not theoretical.  The trap is geography. The trap is logistics. The trap is politics. And once American ground forces  move into this environment, the exit doors start closing very quickly. First, understand the scale  of the battlefield. Iran is an enormous country covering more than 1.6 million square kilometers.  That is larger than Iraq and Afghanistan combined. And anyone who remembers how difficult those  two wars became should pause and consider what that number means for a military occupation. It  means controlling a territory that stretches from scorching deserts to towering mountain chains.  From crowded urban centers to remote villages where supply roads are almost non-existent and  where central authority has always been weak. Even during the 20-year war in Afghanistan, American  forces struggle to control a much smaller country with far fewer people and a far less organized  military. Iran has more than 85 million citizens, a large professional military, and a deep  historical memory of foreign invasion. That memory does not weaken resistance. It hardens it.  Think carefully about how such an invasion will even begin. Where do the troops come from? Where  do they land? Where do they stage the operation and build the supply infrastructure before pushing  forward? The United States cannot simply teleport half a million soldiers into the center of  Iran. They have to deploy somewhere first, establish massive logistical hubs and then  begin pushing forces forward. In 2003, the invasion of Iraq staged largely from Kuwait  and from American bases scattered across the Gulf region. But the political environment today is  dramatically different. Iraq now has a government closely connected with Iran, a government that has  repeatedly stated that Iraqi territory cannot be used as a staging ground for an attack against  its neighbor. If American forces tried to use Iraqi bases for a major invasion, it would trigger  enormous political backlash inside Iraq itself, potentially destabilizing a country that America  spent two decades and trillions of dollars trying to build. Look north and the picture is no more  encouraging. Could American forces stage from Turkey? Technically, Turkey is a NATO member,  which might suggest automatic cooperation. But Ankura has its own complex strategic interests,  and Turkish leadership has consistently shown it will not allow its territory to become a launch  pad for wars that might send Iranian missiles flying toward Turkish cities or destabilize  Turkish trade routes. This means one obvious invasion corridor is restricted and another is  politically unavailable. What remains? Naval forces and Gulf bases. The United States could  launch operations from aircraft carriers in the Persian Gulf and from installations in Qatar and  Bahrain. But this creates an immediate and severe problem. Supply lines. Every single soldier on  a modern battlefield requires constant resupply. food, water, ammunition, spare parts, medical  equipment, fuel, batteries, all of it must travel hundreds or thousands of miles across sea  lanes and through narrow choke points that are extremely vulnerable to disruption. Iran has spent  decades preparing for exactly this scenario. Its military strategy does not require defeating  the United States in a head-on conventional engagement. Instead, the strategy focuses on  making the battlefield so painful, so costly, and so politically unbearable that the invading  force eventually loses the will to continue. Iranian missile forces, drone units, and naval  fast attack boats operating in the shallow waters of the Persian Gulf can target shipping routes  and supply convoys. Even limited disruption forces supply chains to slow down, reroute, or halt  entirely. And when logistics begin breaking down, an army quickly loses its ability to project  force, sustain operations, and maintain the morale of the soldiers doing the fighting. Geography  is where the physical trap becomes impossible to ignore. Look at the western side of Iran on any  topographical map, and you immediately see the massive Zagos mountain range stretching hundreds  of miles across the country. These are not gentle hills. They are steep, rugged mountains  with narrow valleys, unpredictable weather, and countless natural choke points where a small  defending force positioned on high ground can stop a large armored column completely. Heavy  vehicles cannot maneuver freely in this terrain. Convoys are forced onto predictable roads, and  predictability on battlefield is fatal. For more than two decades, Iran has invested heavily  in underground facilities, tunnel networks, and pre-positioned weapons caches throughout  these mountains. Not emergency defenses built in a panic, but deliberate systems constructed with  a patient expectation that one day a foreign army might attempt to cross these passes. Consider what  happens in practical terms when a military convoy enters a narrow mountain pass. The vehicles  stretch out along the road because there is no physical space to maneuver off it. The column  becomes a long vulnerable ribbon of steel. If an ambush destroys the leave vehicles and the rear  vehicles simultaneously, the entire convoy is frozen. Rockets, mortars, and machine gun fire  rain down from the hillsides above, and the attackers disappear back into the terrain before  air support arrives. This pattern of warfare is not hypothetical military theory. Variations  of it occurred repeatedly during the wars in Afghanistan and during every mountain conflict in  modern history. The mountains have always favored the defender. Beyond the Zagos mountains in the  west, the Albor's range dominates the entire northern edge of the country. Some of these peaks  exceed 4,000 meters in elevation. Snow, fog, and extreme cold can ground aircraft and reduce ground  movement to a crawl. Supply lines that already stretch across thousands of miles become even more  fragile in these conditions. Now imagine trying to maintain a continuous supply chain through this  environment while simultaneously facing constant harassment from an enemy that knows every mountain  trail and has prepared defensive positions throughout the high ground. That is not merely  a tactical challenge. It becomes a strategic nightmare that consumes resources, manpower, and  political will at an accelerating pace. While the military struggles with geography and logistics on  the ground, an entirely separate battlefield opens back home. War is not fought only on foreign  soil. Is also fought in the political arena, in the media, in the economic data, and in the  streets of cities far from the fighting. If American casualties begin rising quickly, public  opinion shifts rapidly and unpredictably. The experience of Vietnam showed how a war that began  with broad popular support could fracture American society within a few years as the body count  climbed and the promised victory never arrived. Today, the economic situation in the United  States is already under significant pressure from inflation, housing costs, and stagnant wages  for large parts of the working population. If the government suddenly commits trillions of dollars  to a prolonged ground war in Iran, the political reaction could be explosive. Citizens would  ask loudly and persistently why such enormous resources are being spent destroying a country on  the other side of the world while domestic needs go unmet. The regional dimension adds another  layer of complexity that planners cannot afford to ignore. Iran does not stand alone. It has  developed a network of allied groups and partner organizations across the broader Middle East  over several decades. If American ground forces invade Iran, those networks activate almost  automatically. Missile strikes, drone attacks, and coordinated sabotage operations could target  American military bases across the entire region. Shipping lanes through the Red Sea in the Persian  Gulf could face sustained disruption. Energy infrastructure, refineries, pipelines, power  stations could become active battlefields. The economic consequences would ripple far beyond  the region, affecting global oil prices, supply chains, and financial markets in ways that would  add enormous pressure on every government that had initially supported the campaign. There's also a  subtler trap that often goes unexamined. Foreign militaries historically rely on local alliances  and local intelligence to function effectively in unfamiliar territory. Local partners provide  information about enemy movements, identify sympathetic populations, and guide operations  through complex political landscapes. But those relationships are always fragile and they become  especially unreliable once local actors begin to doubt that the invading force can actually achieve  its objectives. When that doubt takes hold, the information flow can reverse entirely.  Intelligence once provided to the invading force may suddenly start flowing in the other direction.  Convoys get ambushed at precisely the moments when they are most exposed. Patrol routes that seem  secure become fatal. Ford operating bases face relentless rocket and drone harassment. Soldiers  begin to feel that every movement is being watched and every decision anticipated. morale  deteriorates and once morale collapses, even the most sophisticated military equipment cannot  substitute for the human will to keep fighting. Step back from the individual pieces and look  at the entire picture together. Geography limits maneuverability and channels forces into kill  zones. Logistics become dangerously overstretched across thousands of miles of vulnerable supply  routes. Regional actors with their own grievances escalate the conflict beyond anyone's original  planning assumptions. Domestic politics at home begin fracturing under the weight of casualties  and costs. Local alliances become unreliable. Each of these problems would be enormously difficult to  manage on its own. Together, they form a trap that tightens with every passing week. And the deeper  an invading force pushes into Iranian territory, the more politically and militarily difficult it  becomes to withdraw without appearing defeated. This is why serious strategic analysts  consistently describe a potential ground invasion of Iran not as a gamble that might  succeed, but as a commitment that once made becomes almost impossible to exit gracefully.  The invading force always believes that victory is just around the corner. That committing a  little more manpower, a little more equipment, staying just a little longer will finally turn the  situation around. But each additional commitment makes withdrawal more politically difficult.  While the fundamental obstacles, the mountains, the supply lines, the regional blowback, the  domestic opposition remain essentially unchanged. None of this is secret knowledge. Military  planners, intelligence analysts, and political leaders in Washington understand every single one  of these obstacles. They have access to the maps, the logistics models, the historical case studies,  and the classified assessments. The problem is not lack of information. The problem is that  wars often begin anyway, driven by strategic miscalculation, domestic political pressures,  economic incentives, and the peculiarly human tendency to believe that this time will be  different. The real question, therefore, is never simply whether the United States could win  individual battles inside Iran. The real question is whether the entire campaign could escape  the trap that geography, logistics, politics, and regional escalation would immediately  begin constructing around it. Because once those forces start interacting with each other,  events move quickly beyond anyone's control, and the battlefield stops being a place on a map  and becomes something far more dangerous. A system that consumes everything put into it without  producing the outcome anyone originally promised.

full_text: Yeah, we're going live. We're going live, Alexander. We are live with Alexander Mercurus in London. And it is our honor and our great pleasure to have with us on the Duran. First time on the Duran, Professor Chang of the YouTube channel Predictive History, of which I am a huge fan of. a fantastic uh channel on YouTube, a fantastic professor >> uh Professor Chang. Thank you so much for joining us on uh on the Duran and I will have all of uh his channel links in the description box down below. Thank you for joining us, Professor. >> Thank you. >> Fantastic. So, uh let's get started. We have a lot of ground to cover. But uh before we get moving, let's say a quick hello to everyone that is watching us on Odyssey, on Rockfin, on Rumble and YouTube, as well as our locals community, the duran.locals.com, and a big shout out to our moderators as well. Thank you to our moderators in the chat. So, Alexander, Professor Chang, let's uh let's talk about predicting geopolitics. Indeed, because we are, as everybody obviously must know, in a major geopolitical conflict situation at the present time, a geopolitical conflict which was not, in my opinion, design desired by some of the parties to it. I don't think China was set set out a few years ago to find itself catapulted into a geopolitical conflict. I'm absolutely sure that it didn't. The same is true with the Russians. But they are in a geopolitical conflict. And the other major player is the United States and it is the West. And of course all sorts of things are taking place around the world in the Middle East, in Ukraine, in Latin America with all these fleet movements around Venezuela. There's all kinds of events taking place in the worlds of economy and science and technology all the time and all of these interconnect with each other in all sorts of ways. Now on the Duran where we what we tend to do is we tend to go through all of these topics one by one. We we focus on each one and we discuss them Alex and myself and sometimes with our guests in detail. I think what we're going to do today with Professor Chang is something slightly different, which is we're going to try and pull these things together, perhaps looking mostly but not exclusively at the conflict in Europe and the developments that are taking place there in order to try to understand where the final well where these these events are leading us towards. In other words, a more predictive a greater sense of where this is taking us. And there is no one better to do it with than Professor Tang. Um, whose channel I also I should say am a massive fan of and which I absolutely do recommend to everybody to everybody who is viewing us as well. So, Professor Professor Tiang, let's let's begin. We are now I I think you will I think everybody is in agreement that we are in a global geopolitical conflict. There is a view that there are actually two completely different conflicts going on. One between Russia and the United States and the West, the other specifically between China and the United States, but they have to some extent merged. We've had all kinds of interactions between the Americans and the Chinese recently and between the Russians and the Chinese. It's not been noticed so much in the West, but there are underlying trends here. There are events that are shaping how things are going. Perhaps you could take us a little bit further and discuss. Should we start with Ukraine and the conflict there? the first big war in Europe, the first war involving major clashes of armies that has taken place since the end of the Second World War. Europe has historically in the West at least been the pivot area. It's probably not that to anything like the same degree that it used to be. But can us can you perhaps give us a few thoughts about where you think this is going? may be looking a bit of um predictive history if I can put it like this. You know, looking back at history, looking also perhaps at uh um other um scientific tools which I know you're very skilled at using. >> Okay. So, thanks so much for having me on the Duran. I I've been a big fan of your show for like four years now. I actually started first watching the Duran when the war broke out in uh on February uh 22nd, 2022. And the reason why was all the like mainstream news coverage I was watching was like, "Oh my god, Ukraine's going to march to Moscow and Putin is going to fall and the Russian army um is incompetent." And I couldn't believe what I was watching because it absolutely made no sense to me. Um I'm not sure if you remember the ghost of Kiev um um where you know this lone fighter jet was flying around Ukraine just shooting on all these um um Russian jets. So the news coverage at that time was literally driving me insane. It it made actually no sense to me. It it was it was as though I was living in a I was living in a bizarre world.
